content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 16, 2009


Jeff Preach
31744 Castaic Road, Suite 201
Castaic, CA 91384

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-194

Dear Mr. Preach:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) In addition, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

May you participate in Castaic Area Town Council recommendations regarding possible sites for a high school when you have an ownership interest in real property directly across the street from one of the sites being considered?

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the town council is solely advisory in nature and does not exercise decision making authority. Therefore, you are not considered a public official under the Act and are not covered by the Act's conflict-of-interest disqualification provisions.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-194
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are one of 10 Castaic Area Town Council members. The council is an elected
advisory board that makes recommendations and provides community perspectives to the
Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors regarding development and other issues
involving Castaic and nearby communities in the unincorporated area of Los Angeles
County (the "County"). Town council members are not required to file Statements of
Economic Interests with the County.

You have a one-third interest in 52 acres of vacant parcel directly across the street
from one of several possible high school sites being considered for development. You
wish to participate in decisions making recommendations regarding the proposed site for
the high school.

During our telephone conversation of September 8, 2009, you stated that the town
council would make a recommendation to the board of supervisors, which in turn would
present its recommendations to the Castaic Union School District and its board of
trustees. The school district and its board make the final decision regarding the proposed
site for the high school.

You also stated that the town council's recommendations regarding the school site
would be in a letter to the board of supervisors. In the letter, the town council would
recommend three school sites in the order of preference.

In the past, the town council's recommendations have been approved "nearly
100%" of the time (but with substantive modifications by the board of supervisors) with
only one exception involving the location of a convenience store. You stated in that case,
the town council supported the project and the board of supervisors ultimately voted to
reject it.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.

File No. A-09-194
Page No. 3


**Step 1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100?**

The Act defines the term "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . ." (Section 82048.)

The term "public official" is further defined by Regulation 18701(a)(1):

"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048, which defines
'public official,' and Government Code section 82019, which defines
'designated employee,' the following definitions apply:

(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authority."

Under Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A) a board or commission possesses
decision making authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

(ii) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridden; or

(iii) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an
extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by another public official or
governmental agency."

If the town council meets any of the tests of Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(i), (ii) or
(iii), it possesses decisionmaking authority, and its members would be considered public
officials who are subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.

Alternatively, if the town council does not have decisionmaking authority under
regulation 18701(a)(1), the committee's members are not considered public officials
under the Act and are not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules solely by virtue of
their membership on the town council.

According to the facts you have provided, the town council is purely an advisory
committee whose task is to provide community input and recommendations to the board
of supervisors regarding community development and other local issues. The recommendations
presented to the board of supervisors are subject to review and substantial modification
by the board. In addition, you stated in our September 8, 2009 conversation that with

File No. A-09-194
Page No. 4

regard to the high school site decision, the town council does not have an exclusive
power to initiate a decision nor does it have power to veto a decision, which may not be
overridden. The final decision rests with the Castaic Union School District and its board
of trustees.

You have indicated town council members do not have decision making authority
and do not make any final governmental decisions (Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)), or
compel or prevent a governmental decision (Regulation 18701(a)(1)(B)). Furthermore,
your facts indicate that the members are not included in the county's conflict-of-interest
code and are not subject to the Act's disclosure provisions.

Advisory Bodies:

Even if it is determined that a commission serves in a solely advisory function,
Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(iii) also requires an assessment of the impact of an advisory
body's recommendations by analyzing the extent to which its recommendations have
been followed in the past.

If there is a history of a particular advisory body's recommendations being
routinely accepted without amendment or modification, the body converts from a solely
advisory function to one making or participating in the making of a governmental
decision, and its members are considered public officials subject to the governmental
conflict-of-interest provisions under the Act.

Your letter and our telephone conversation of September 8, 2009 indicate that the
town council members have been making recommendations for about 10 years and that
nearly all their recommendations have been accepted, but with substantive modifications
by the board of supervisors. Therefore, the town council has not made recommendations
over a period of time that have been regularly approved without significant amendment
or modification by another public official or governmental agency. (Regulation
18701(a)(1)(C).)

Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the town council is solely
advisory in nature and does not exercise decision making authority.²

Accordingly, the town council's members are not currently considered public
officials under the Act. Therefore, they are not covered by the Act's conflict-of-interest
disqualification provisions.

² This conclusion is based on the facts provided. Note that if the town council's recommendations
are routinely accepted without substantive amendment or modification, the body would not be considered
solely advisory and its members are considered public officials subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions
under the Act.

File No. A-09-194
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl
